messages.content: Big day. I've been tracing hoses and I think I finally understand the brake system on the truck. The vacuum booster setup I've been planning might be wrong.
Tell me what you found. Walk me through the hose routing and what you're seeing physically.

I pulled the FSM Group 5 section on brakes. The truck has a HYDRAULIC BRAKE BOOSTER from the factory. It's called hydroboost. The power steering pump supplies pressure to it. That black box on the firewall I kept calling 'the vacuum thing' — that's the hydroboost unit.

That's a significant discovery. Let me pull the FSM reference.

FSM Group 5, Brakes → Power Brake Assist → Hydraulic Brake Booster → Description and Operation:
"Vehicles equipped with the diesel engine use a Hydraulic Booster to supply power assist to the brake system. The booster is mounted to the front cowl panel and the master cylinder is mounted to the booster. The hydraulic pressure is supplied to the booster from the power steering pump."

This changes everything. The vacuum pump on this truck serves HVAC only — not brakes. And that blue cylindrical component you photographed? That's the hydroboost accumulator. High-pressure nitrogen gas inside. Critical: do not drop it on the accumulator.

The accumulator gives you 2-3 emergency brake applications if the PS pump fails.

Major impact on the build plan: the $1,877 hydroboost conversion line item you had in the brake system overhaul — you already have one. That drops the brake system cost from ~$4,815 to ~$2,938.
I traced all four PS hose runs today. Physical cut-matching, confirmed endpoints. Can you document the complete circuit?

Here's the complete circuit from your physical trace:

Run 1: Gear box port 1 (long metal) → Hydroboost right port. Pressure out to gear. Hose in hand.
Run 2: Pump bottom port (longest metal) → Hydroboost top port. Pressure in from pump. Hose in hand.
Run 3: Pump top port (hose to metal tip) → Hydroboost 2nd/forward-right port. Booster return to pump. Hose in hand.
Run 4: Pump middle port (med metal) → Gear box port 2 (short metal). Gear return to pump. Missing — cut twice months apart.

Circuit: PRESSURE IN: Pump bottom → Hydroboost top. PRESSURE OUT: Hydroboost right → Gear box port 1. RETURN (booster): Hydroboost 2nd right → Pump top. RETURN (gear box): Gear box port 2 → [cooler?] → Pump middle.

The HVAC vacuum line is a completely separate system — vacuum pump thin nipple → TEE junction at firewall → HVAC door actuators. It was never cut. Only serves HVAC.

FSM notes: hydroboost pressure line fittings torque to 28 Nm (21 ft-lbs), check O-rings before install. Mounting nuts also 28 Nm. Steering gear box hose fittings: 31 Nm (23 ft-lbs).

One open question from the FSM: Group 5 specifies MOPAR PS fluid, not ATF, for hydroboost systems. Need to verify ATF+4 compatibility before filling.
And the Borgeson pump — I mounted it but haven't torqued it yet.

Borgeson 800328 is seated on the vacuum pump, mounting studs not yet torqued. Note for tomorrow: the oil pressure sending unit on the driver side of the block, just behind the PS pump — 2-pin connector threaded into a pressurized oil gallery. It interfered with your pump installation angle. Do NOT remove it — oil pressure everywhere if you do. The factory pump clears it; Borgeson 800328 clears it once fully seated.

facts: The 1997 Ram 2500 Cummins has factory hydroboost braking, not a vacuum booster
Hydroboost is mounted to the firewall behind the master cylinder and receives pressure from the power steering pump
The hydroboost accumulator is a blue cylindrical component containing high-pressure nitrogen gas
The accumulator provides 2-3 emergency brake applications if the PS pump fails
All four power steering hose runs have been physically traced and confirmed
Run 4 (gear box return to pump) is missing and was cut twice months apart
The HVAC vacuum line is intact and was never cut; it serves HVAC door actuators only
Hydroboost pressure line fittings torque to 28 Nm (21 ft-lbs) with O-ring inspection required
Hydroboost mounting nuts torque to 28 Nm (21 ft-lbs)
Steering gear box hose fittings torque to 31 Nm (23 ft-lbs)
Borgeson 800328 PS pump is mounted but studs are not yet torqued
FSM Group 5 specifies MOPAR PS fluid, not ATF, for hydroboost systems
Oil pressure sending unit is on the driver side of the block behind the PS pump and must not be removed
decisions: Remove the $1,877 hydroboost conversion line item from the brake system overhaul because the truck already has factory hydroboost
contradictions: Previous assumption was that truck had vacuum braking; discovered it has factory hydraulic braking (hydroboost) from the power steering pump
The black box on the firewall was previously misidentified as a vacuum unit; it is the hydroboost unit
The blue cylindrical component was previously misidentified as a vacuum check valve; it is the hydroboost accumulator

metadata.notes: Sourced from akron/memory/2026-02-10.md — factory hydroboost discovery and PS circuit mapping
